D6 Display L5 Evaluate Reference

DLP & LCoS Projection Engines

a chip of tilting micromirrors paints grey by flicking thousands of times a second — and colour by spinning a wheel past the beam

A DLP chip is a grid of micromirrors, each tilting ±12° to send light either to the screen or to a dump. Grey is time: a mirror flicks on and off, and its duty cycle sets brightness. A single-chip DLP makes colour with a spinning RGB wheel, flashing the primaries in sequence — fast eyes catch a "rainbow" break-up. LCoS instead modulates a reflective liquid-crystal layer.

Set a grey level and watch the mirror's PWM on-time grow. Switch engines: DLP cycles R, G, B through the colour wheel; LCoS holds all three continuously on three panels. Note where each loses light and where colour break-up can appear.
